name: chambercourt

metadata.name: ChamberCourt
metadata.summary: Simple 2D grid game framework based on PyGame

documentation:
# ChamberCourt

https://github.com/rrthomas/chambercourt  

by Reuben Thomas <rrt@sc3d.org>  

ChamberCourt is a framework for simple 2-D grid-based games, based on
[PyGame](https://pygame.org).

It is only intended for use by the author, mostly to resurrect a bunch of
old games he wrote a long time ago. As such, there is no documentation other
than the code. However, if anyone would like to help make it usable by
others, I would be delighted to hear from them. I would prefer to keep it as
simple as possible; however there is room for improvement in many areas. For
example, it would be nice if the games could run on the web (I have
experimented with [pygbag](https://github.com/pygame-web/pygbag/), but I
didn’t get very far yet), and some basic functionality would be nice such as
a UI for rebinding keys.

The package is named after the central courtyard of my school, Winchester
College, because I wrote this package first while resurrecting a game,
[WinColl](https://github.com/rrthomas/wincoll), named after the school.


## Installation and use
 
Install with `pip`: `pip install chambercourt`.

A demonstration front-end is available, called `chambercourt`. You can run
it directly from a Git checkout with `PYTHONPATH=. python -m chambercourt`.


## Creating and editing levels

Levels are created and edited in [Tiled](https://www.mapeditor.org/). All
in-game graphics and sounds are stored in the levels directory by
convention. A demonstration level is in `chambercourt/levels`.

Some notes about level design:

+ A set of levels is numbered according to the lexical order of their file
  names.
+ Levels need exactly one start position, given by placing the hero.


## Copyright and Disclaimer

ChamberCourt is distributed under the GNU Public License version 3, or, at
your option, any later version. See the file COPYING.

THIS PROGRAM IS PROVIDED AS IS, WITH NO WARRANTY. USE IS AT THE USER'S RISK.
Except for the files mentioned below, the package is copyright Reuben
Thomas.

The font “Acorn Mode 1”, which is based on the design of Acorn computers’
system font, is by p1.mark and Reuben Thomas and licensed under CC BY-SA
3.0.

The death buzzer sound effect was adapted from
[Buzzer sounds (Wrong answer/Error) by Breviceps](https://freesound.org/s/493163)
under CC 0.

modules.name: chambercourt.langdetect
documentation:
Detect the user's preferred language for messages.

Uses the standard library's `locale` module, except on macOS and Windows,
where custom code is used.

functions.name: chambercourt.langdetect.language_code
documentation:
Returns the user's environment language as a language code string.
(examples: 'en_GB', 'en_US', 'pt_PT', 'pt_BR', etc.)

functions.name: chambercourt.langdetect._lang_code_mac
documentation:
Returns the user's language preference as defined in the Language & Region
preference pane in macOS's System Preferences.

Uses the shell command `defaults read -g AppleLocale` that prints out a
language code to standard output. Assumptions about the command:
- It exists and is in the shell's PATH.
- It accepts those arguments.
- It returns a usable language code.

Reference documentation:
- The man page for the `defaults` command on macOS.
- The macOS underlying API:
  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/foundation/nsuserdefaults.

classes.name: chambercourt.game.Tile
documentation: We would like to use StrEnum + auto, but aenum does not support
extend_enum on StrEnums.

methods.name: chambercourt.game.Game.splurge
documentation: Fill the game area with one sprite.

methods.name: chambercourt.game.Game.title_screen
documentation: Show instructions and choose start level.

modules.name: chambercourt.ptext
documentation: ptext module: place this in your import directory.
ptext.draw(text, pos=None, **options)
Please see README.md for explanation of options.
https://github.com/cosmologicon/pygame-text

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._binarysearch
documentation: Return the largest integer in the range [xmin, xmax] such that f(x) is True.

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._resolvecolor
documentation: Returns the color as a color RGB or RGBA tuple (i.e. 3 or 4 integers in the range 0-255)
If color is None, fall back to the default. If default is also None, return None.
Both color and default can be a list, tuple, a color name, an HTML color format string, a hex
number string, or an integer pixel value. See pygame.Color constructor for specification.

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._rotatesurf
documentation: Rotate the given surface by the given angle, in degrees.
If angle is an exact multiple of 90, use pygame.transform.rotate, otherwise fall back to
pygame.transform.rotozoom.

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._fadesurf
documentation: Apply the given alpha value to a copy of the Surface.

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._splitbytags
documentation: Splits a string into substrings with corresponding tag specs.
Empty strings are skipped. Consecutive identical tag specs are not merged.
e.g. if tagspec0.underline = False and underlinetag = "_" then:
_splitbytags("_abc__def_ ghi_") yields three items:
("abc", TagSpec(underline=True))
("def", TagSpec(underline=True))
(" ghi", TagSpec(underline=False))

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._breaktext
documentation: Finds the last valid breakpoint in the line of text. A breakpoint is a position at which the line
can be split without improperly breaking words.
Returns (breaktext, breakpoint)

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._wrapline
documentation: Split a single line of text.
textandtags is the output of _splitbytags, i.e. a sequence of (string, tag spec) tuples.

functions.name: chambercourt.ptext._blitpos
documentation: The actual position on the screen where the surf is to be blitted, rather than the specified
anchor position.

variables.name: chambercourt.ptext.DEFAULT_ALIGN
documentation: left, center, or right

variables.name: chambercourt.ptext.DEFAULT_ANCHOR
documentation: 0, 0 = top left ;  1, 1 = bottom right

classes.name: chambercourt.ptext._Options

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._Options.key
documentation: For cached function calls, this is a hashable representation of the options object. Assumes
that all field values are either hashable, or dicts whose keys are comparable and values are
hashable.

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._Options.resolvetags
documentation: The following methods are just put here for code deduplication. A couple different functions
use a lot of the same code.
Options objects encapsulate the keyword arguments to functions that take a lot of optional keyword
arguments.
Options object base class. Subclass for Options objects specific to different functions.
Specify valid fields in the _fields list. All keyword fields are optional. Unspecified fields
default to None, unless otherwise specified in the _defaults list.

classes.name: chambercourt.ptext._DrawOptions

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._DrawOptions.expandposition
documentation: Expand each 2-element position specifier and overwrite the corresponding 1-element
position specifiers.

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._DrawOptions.expandanchor
documentation: Update the pos and anchor fields, if unspecified, to be specified by the positional
keyword arguments.

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._DrawOptions.resolvesurf
documentation: Unspecified surf values default to the display surface.
Options argument for the draw function. Specifies both text styling and positioning.

classes.name: chambercourt.ptext._LayoutOptions
documentation: Options for the layout function. By design, this has the same options as draw, although some of
them are silently ignored.

classes.name: chambercourt.ptext.TagSpec
documentation: Tracks everything that can be updated by tags.

classes.name: chambercourt.ptext._Span

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._Span.__init__
documentation: Phase 1: set by _wrapline

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._Span.setlayout
documentation: Phase 2: set by _wrap

methods.name: chambercourt.ptext._Span.setdetails
documentation: Phase 3: set by getsurf
These are not required to determine layout or position, only for rendering.
The _Span class tracks many attributes of a single span of text, i.e. a string of text within a
single line that has a single font and TagSpec. That is, a single span corresponds to a single
call to font.render.
This is not a clean abstraction, and some of the state of this object only makes sense in the
context of the overall draw call. At various stages of the call, some of the fields will not yet
be populated.
